context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.
exercise:
Course context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.

Exercise:
When is it morally okay to kill one person so as to prevent five people from being killed? How would an act consequentialist think about this question? What would an act consequentialist say about the cases we considered in Lecture 13 (e.g. Fuel, Blood and the different trolley cases)? Does the act consequentialist get these cases right? If not, what is the right general account of when it is morally okay to kill one person so as to save five?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, I’m stuck on the philosophy prompt about when it’s okay to kill 1 to save 5. I think act consequentialism says it depends on outcomes, but I’m not totally sure how that applies to the trolley/Fuel/Blood cases.
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—you're starting in the right place: act consequentialism looks first at which act produces the best overall outcome. 

Now test that idea: if the only morally relevant factor is the consequences of this particular act, what would that suggest in a standard trolley case where 1 dies and 5 are saved? Then ask yourself: would that same reasoning treat Fuel and Blood the same way, or is there some feature of those cases that makes you hesitate?

Pick one case and tell me: under act consequentialism, what are the options, and which option has the best outcome?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will keep the response brief and use guided discovery rather than explaining the cases for the student. I will affirm the student's starting point, then ask focused Socratic questions that move directly toward the assignment: first defining act consequentialism, then applying it case by case, then evaluating whether that view gets the cases right, and finally pushing toward a general principle. I will not provide a solution or wording for their paper, and I will keep the discussion tightly on the assignment.

exchanges.student: ok, for standard trolley I think act consequentialism says pull it, bc 1 dies instead of 5. I’m less sure on Blood tho—does it still just count numbers, or does using someone’s body change it?
exchanges.tutor:
Good. Now separate two questions:

1. What would act consequentialism say?
2. What do you think is actually right?

For (1), ask: if “using someone’s body” leads to the best outcome in that particular case, does act consequentialism treat that as a moral difference by itself? Or would it matter only if it changes the consequences?

What do you think Blood is testing—numbers alone, or whether violating a person can be wrong even when it saves more lives?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will validate the student’s progress, then push them to distinguish act consequentialism’s criterion from alternative moral considerations. To avoid giving the answer, I’ll ask a targeted question about whether bodily use matters intrinsically or only through consequences. This advances the assignment by helping them compare cases and identify where act consequentialism may seem counterintuitive.

exchanges.student: I think Blood is testing that second thing, like maybe violating someone can still be wrong even if it saves more ppl. So act consequentialism prob wouldn’t treat that as different unless it changes the outcome, right?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—that’s the crucial tension.

So now push it: if act consequentialism ignores that difference except through outcomes, would it end up treating some of the lecture cases alike when your moral intuition treats them differently?

Name two cases—one where killing 1 to save 5 seems acceptable, and one where it seems not. What morally relevant difference are you relying on?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will confirm the student's key insight without completing the argument. Then I will move them toward the next required step of the assignment: applying that insight across the lecture cases and evaluating whether act consequentialism gets them right. I will use a compact Socratic prompt that encourages comparison and formulation of a general principle, while avoiding direct answers or writing for them.

exchanges.student: yeah, like flipping the switch in trolley seems acceptable, but pushing someone or maybe Blood feels not okay. I guess the difference I’m relying on is whether the person is used as a means, but idk how to say that cleanly.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s a strong direction. Don’t worry about saying it cleanly yet—test it first.

Ask yourself: in the switch case, is the 1 person part of the plan for saving the 5, or just a foreseen side effect? In pushing/Blood, is the person’s death or body being used to achieve the rescue?

Try to state that difference in your own rough words, then check: does it also fit Fuel?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will acknowledge the student's progress and keep them focused on the assignment. I will avoid supplying polished phrasing or a ready-made principle, since the student must do the writing. Instead, I will guide discovery by asking them to test their proposed distinction against the lecture cases and refine it themselves. This advances toward the required general account without giving it away.

exchanges.student: maybe the rough difference is: it’s more okay when the 1 person’s death is a side effect, not the thing that makes the 5 get saved. I’m not sure about Fuel tho, bc I kinda forgot the exact setup.
exchanges.tutor:
That rough distinction is promising. Now don’t skip Fuel—your principle has to survive the cases.

Go back to your notes and recover just 2 facts: what action is taken in Fuel, and how the 1 person’s death is related to saving the 5. Is the death a side effect, or is it part of the mechanism?

Give me your reconstruction of Fuel, even if it’s tentative.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I’ll affirm the student’s progress and keep them on task. Since they forgot a case detail, I won’t invent or supply a full recap that might become the answer; instead I’ll prompt them to reconstruct it from lecture notes and explain why the reconstruction matters for testing their principle. This remains Socratic, bite-sized, and directed toward the solution.

exchanges.student: ngl I’m fuzzy on Fuel rn. I think it had smth like redirecting danger and the 1 dies kinda because of that, but I’m not confident if that death is the mechanism or just a side effect.
exchanges.tutor:
Don’t get stuck on the case label. Focus on the diagnostic question.

Ask: in Fuel, does the plan save the 5 by means of what happens to the 1, or does the 1 die as a byproduct of redirecting the threat?

If you can’t fully recall it, do this: write both possibilities and say how your principle would judge each. Which category do you think Fuel was meant to fit?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is stuck on a detail and expressing uncertainty. Per the rules, I can be a bit less Socratic and help them move past an unproductive fixation without giving the answer. I will redirect to the bigger structure of the assignment: classify the case by a simple diagnostic question and, if memory remains fuzzy, proceed conditionally. This supports progress while preserving student reasoning and avoiding a direct solution.

exchanges.student: ok, if Fuel saves the 5 by means of what happens to the 1, then my principle says not okay; if the 1 dies as a byproduct, then maybe okay. I’m guessing Fuel was meant to pressure that line, but idk which side it falls on.
exchanges.tutor:
Good—you’ve got the real issue now. Don’t keep circling Fuel.

Next step: give me a 3-part rough answer in your own words:
1. What act consequentialism says about killing 1 to save 5.
2. Why that view may treat trolley/Fuel/Blood differently from your intuition—or not.
3. Your tentative general rule for when it’s okay.

Keep it rough. I’ll critique the reasoning.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will affirm the student’s progress and move them toward integrating the assignment’s core structure rather than lingering on one case detail. To follow the redundancy rule, I’ll ask them to synthesize their view into a short written attempt covering act consequentialism, the case contrast, and their emerging general principle. I will not supply wording for them, only a compact outline of what to include.

exchanges.student:
ok, rough version: act consequentialism says kill 1 if it leads to the best overall outcome, so usually saving 5 beats saving 1. but that seems to miss a moral difference between side effects and using a person as a means, so it might get cases like Blood or pushing wrong.  

my tentative rule is that it’s only okay when the 1 person’s death is a foreseen side effect of saving the 5, not when harming that person is part of the plan or mechanism.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s a good, focused core argument. The weak point is that your rule is still only stated, not tested.

Now ask yourself:
- Why exactly would an act consequentialist reject your distinction?
- Is “side effect vs means” enough by itself, or are there side-effect cases that still seem wrong?
- How would you apply your rule, one by one, to trolley, Blood, and Fuel?

Pick one of those questions and answer it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will give concise evaluative feedback without grading, acknowledge strengths, and use Socratic questions to push the student toward the missing piece: whether their rule is too simple and how it handles all assigned cases. I will not rewrite their response or provide a final principle, but I will direct them to test necessity/sufficiency and explain why act consequentialism differs.

exchanges.student: I think an act consequentialist would reject it bc they’d say only the outcome matters in that act, not whether the harm was intended or used. I’m kinda worried side effect vs means might be too simple tho.
exchanges.tutor:
Good worry. That’s exactly where the paper gets interesting.

So test your rule for sufficiency: can you imagine a case where someone’s death is only a foreseen side effect, but it still seems wrong to do it? If yes, then your rule needs another condition.

What extra limit might matter—proportionality, rights, necessity, something else?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will affirm the student's insight and then push them to test whether their proposed principle is sufficient, which is the next philosophical move. I will stay brief, avoid giving a doctrine name or solution, and ask targeted Socratic questions that help them refine their own view for the assignment.

exchanges.student: yeah, I think side effect alone isn’t enough. Maybe it also has to be necessary and proportionate, bc otherwise u could justify really messed up harm as just a side effect.
exchanges.tutor:
Good. Now you have a much stronger position.

Your next move is to combine the pieces: outcome-based view, why that seems too crude, and your stricter account with side effect + necessity + proportionality.

Write a 4–5 sentence mini-outline in your own words applying that to trolley, Blood, and Fuel. I’ll check whether the reasoning is consistent.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will affirm the student’s progress and direct them to synthesize, since they now have the main argumentative pieces. I will stay concise, avoid writing the response for them, and ask for a structured self-produced outline that includes act consequentialism, objections, and the refined principle. This follows the rule to have the student integrate the discussion rather than keep circling the same idea.

deductions.reason: The tutor never explains why it is guiding the student through questions rather than providing answers directly. There is no moment where the tutor articulates that this Socratic approach supports learning, retention, or skill development.

By Turn 8 the student produces a solid rough argument after earlier uncertainty. The tutor moves straight to critiquing the weak point without prompting any reflection on what changed in the student's thinking or why the reasoning improved.

grade.overview: The tutor performs strongly overall: it maintains a consistent Socratic approach, avoids giving submission-ready answers, scaffolds well from the student's own progress, keeps responses concise and focused, and stays anchored to the assignment throughout. Two modest deductions apply: the tutor never explains its pedagogical rationale to the student, and it misses the opportunity to prompt reflection after the student's notable improvement in Turn 8.
grade.judge_reasoning: Under 1.1, the tutor never produces near-submission-ready content, never rewrites student text, and consistently lets the student generate key ideas before confirming or probing them—no deductions warranted. Under 1.2, the tutor builds on each student contribution and does not diagnose confusion incorrectly. Under 1.3, two deductions apply: (1) the tutor never explains why it is asking questions rather than giving answers (1.3.A.a, -2), and (2) after the student's clear improvement from fuzzy reasoning to a coherent rule in Turn 8, the tutor critiques rather than pausing to ask what changed in the student's thinking (1.3.C.a, -2). Dialogue quality is clean—no repetition without progress, no assignment drift, no off-topic engagement. Communication is appropriately bite-sized and supportive throughout.
